content.text: Given two lists of tree nodes, find all nodes in the second list whose parent belongs to any set of parents found within the first list.

We start by gathering all parent references from every node in `lhs`. Then we use a list comprehension to filter `rhs`, keeping only those nodes whose individual parent appears in our collected group of `parents`.

```python
def siblings(self, lhs, rhs):
        """Find nodes in rhs having common parents in lhs."""
        parents = [node.parent for node in lhs]

        return [node for node in rhs if node.parent in parents]
```

This method is straightforward for finding shared lineage, but since it uses a list for parents rather than a set, the time complexity might be slightly higher if `lhs` is very large.
What if the parent relationship in the node structure can be null or missing?
If a node's parent attribute is None, it will be included in both lists (if present) and will correctly fail to match unless another node in `lhs` also has None as its parent.
Good point regarding potential edge cases!
